title: Filing a Final Tax Return for Someone Who Died
summary: A final tax return is generally required after a death, and knowing the basics removes some of the mystery.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Filing a Final Tax Return for Someone Who Died | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: A final tax return is generally required after a death, and knowing the basics removes some of the mystery.
whatYouNeedToKnow: A final individual tax return covers income up to the date of death.
The executor or surviving spouse typically files it.
A separate estate tax return may be needed if the estate itself earns income afterward.

paragraphs: A final Form 1040 is generally required, covering the person's income from the start of the tax year through their date of death, filed by the executor or a surviving spouse, often on the normal tax deadline the following year.

If the estate itself continues to earn income after death — from investments, rental property, or a business — a separate estate income tax return may also be required, which is a different filing from the person's final personal return.

nextSteps: Identify who will file the final personal return — usually the executor or surviving spouse.
Gather income records for the year up to the date of death.
Ask a tax professional whether a separate estate return is also needed.
disclaimer: General information, not tax advice — a tax professional or accountant can confirm specific filing requirements.
